steps.title: Target Heller's claim
steps.content: Heller's hypothesis is that Calusa watercourts had an additional purpose: fostering conch. The question asks for the finding that would most directly weaken that hypothesis. The strongest evidence would show that the enclosures were not actually suited to maintaining the conditions conch or similar sea snails need to thrive.

steps.title: Separate weaker evidence
steps.content: Choice A would support Heller because it finds greater marine diversity, especially among sea snails, inside watercourts than in food-preparation areas. Choice B discusses changes in open coastal waters, not whether watercourts could foster conch. Choice D says conch shells are not the majority in most sediment layers, but that does not prove the design was unsuitable for conch.

steps.title: Use the design evidence
steps.content: Choice C directly attacks the physical feasibility of Heller's claim. If radar surveys show the watercourts could sustain fish but could not maintain shallow, seagrass-rich environments where sea snails thrive, then the enclosures were poorly suited to foster conch. The answer is C.
